# 🇱🇰 Sri Lanka This Week

![LastUpdated](https://img.shields.io/badge/last_updated-2026--04--15_09:53:57-green)
[![RSS](https://img.shields.io/badge/RSS-Feed-orange)](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nuuuwan/lk_news_digest/refs/heads/main/rss.xml)

*Generated by gpt-5 from 98 English News Articles published between 2026-04-08 and 2026-04-14.*

## 1. Sri Lanka secures staff-level deal for $700m tranche under IMF programme

The agreement covers the combined fifth and sixth reviews and would take total disbursements to about $2.4bn 💰, pending consideration by the Executive Board. Programme documents note steady macro stabilisation, with low single‑digit inflation and stronger reserves, while underscoring that completion hinges on continued reform implementation.

## 2. Colombo Stock Exchange logs a broad rally with ASPI up 4.21% and S&P SL20 up 4.42% on April 8

Turnover reached Rs. 6.61bn with 11 negotiated crossings, including sizeable prints in banking and diversified holdings. The rupee was quoted around Rs. 315.10/25 per US dollar in spot trade, and dealers reported lower bond yields across key tenors, as daily share volume exceeded 240m units.

## 3. Government unveils Rs. 2.16bn modernisation of Colombo Fort Railway Station with ADB support

The 15‑month upgrade introduces automated ticketing, lifts and overhead bridges for accessibility, and new passenger facilities configured to international standards. Works are being carried out under the Transport, Highways and Urban Development portfolio to reconfigure the island’s busiest node as a safer, technology‑enabled hub.

## 4. Commercial Bank teams up with NCG Express to roll out fully digital ticketing across private buses

Conductors are being equipped with handheld devices to accept contactless cards, wallets and QR scans, with instant confirmations and automated settlement over the acquiring network. Initial routes have commenced under a structured rollout, enabling cash‑free fares and producing auditable records that standardise high‑volume, everyday transactions.

## 5. Train services on the Northern Line resume after cyclone repairs funded by an Indian grant

Track restoration under a $5m package was completed ahead of Avurudu, reconnecting stretches damaged by Ditwah and re‑establishing through movements to key northern towns. The reopening reinstates a critical long‑distance corridor for passengers and freight, easing road congestion during the festive peak.

## 6. Hayleys Solar completes 800kW rooftop installation for LSEG’s Sri Lanka campus

The six‑building array of 1,412 high‑efficiency panels is expected to generate roughly 1,250 MWh a year, covering close to 80% of daytime demand and avoiding about 850 tonnes of CO₂ annually ⚡. Engineering included extensive underground cabling across an active site, executed over two months with a zero‑accident record.

## 7. Vidullanka acquires 100% of Bhoruka Power Lanka, adding the 4.5MW Gurugoda mini‑hydro to its portfolio

The buyer now counts 26 operating assets after the Aranayake plant joins a fleet that spans hydro, solar and emerging storage projects. Recent activity includes four ground‑mounted solar commissions, a tender win for a 50MW wind project and a 10MW BESS, plus the lowest‑bid outcome for a mini‑hydro in Fiji.

## 8. Control Union Sri Lanka sets two global firsts with inaugural Deforestation‑Free Standard certificates

The auditor issued the maiden DFS credential to Hapugasthenna Plantations PLC, covering eleven rubber estates assessed for legality, labour safeguards and land‑use verification aligned to EU rules. A subsequent Group Certificate to Samson Rubber Industries created a shared structure that brings smallholders under one compliance umbrella while preserving traceability to plot.

## 9. Motor Traffic Commissioner General further remanded to 20 April over alleged fraudulent vehicle registration

The suspect was produced before the Pilessa Magistrate’s Court in Kurunegala, extending custody in a case involving an altered chassis on a cab. Investigators from the Criminal Investigation Department arrested the official on 27 March and have filed aiding‑and‑abetting charges tied to the registration process.

## 10. Colombo Dockyard announces board changes as Capt. (Retd.) Jagmohan becomes Chairman after MDL takes 51%

The shipbuilder confirmed Thimira S. Godakumbura continuing as Managing Director/CEO, with new non‑executive appointments including Vish Govindasamy and Senthilverl Nandhanan. The Indian majority owner’s nominee directors add naval architecture and yard leadership credentials to the boardroom, aligning governance with a post‑acquisition growth mandate.

## Model Prompt (for [gpt-5](https://platform.openai.com/docs/models/gpt-5))

```
You will be given several Sri Lankan news articles from the past week. Write a concise, cohesive editorial digest in the style of *The Economist*, output as a JSON list. Each list element must be a dict of the form:

```json
{
  "title": "...",
  "body": "..."
}
```

**Length:** The complete digest should contain exactly 10 items. Each item should have a body of 2–3 sentences, resulting in a total of roughly 800–1000 words across all body paragraphs combined.

**Titles and body as a single continuous narrative:** Write each item so that the title is the opening clause and the body continues directly from it — together they form one unbroken piece of prose. No fact, figure, or proper noun introduced in the title should be repeated in the body. The body picks up where the title leaves off, adding new information only.

*Example of the intended style:*
> **Title:** Sri Lanka and the IMF reach a staff-level agreement on the fifth and sixth reviews of the four-year Extended Fund Facility
> **Body:** The deal unlocks roughly $700m, lifting total disbursements under the programme to approximately $2.4bn 💰, subject to Executive Board approval.

**Titles** must read like headlines from a reputable newspaper (think *Financial Times* or *The Economist*) — precise, active, and newsy — but when read in sequence, they should form a narrative arc that shows how the week's events connect and build on one another. Titles must contain only verifiable facts, no opinion or editorialising.

**Body** paragraphs must add context and nuance beyond the headline. Do not use bullet points. Explain any non-obvious acronyms or jargon on first use. Use emojis purposefully and sparingly where they aid scannability (e.g. 💰 for fiscal matters, ⚡ for energy).

**Strict factual standard — apply to every sentence:**

- Include only **events that occurred** (votes taken, laws passed, figures released, agreements signed, arrests made, prices recorded) and **objective facts** (statistics, measurements, named parties to a transaction, official outcomes).
- **Exclude entirely:** statements, quotes, promises, warnings, predictions, denials, calls for action, expressions of concern, and any other speech acts — even from officials or institutions. The fact that someone *said* something is not itself a fact about the world.
- If the only available information on a topic is what someone claimed or announced, omit that topic rather than report the claim as fact.
- Do not speculate, editorialize, or characterise intent or motivation.

**Causality standard — apply to every title and sentence:**

- Report events as separate, independent facts. Do not imply that one event caused another unless a direct causal mechanism is part of the established, measurable record.
- Avoid constructions such as "as", "following", "amid", "boosting", "driven by", or "in response to" that suggest one event caused or coincided meaningfully with another. Multiple factors almost always contribute to any outcome, and implying a single cause is a form of editorialising.
- If two events occurred in the same week, report them separately. Do not place them in the same sentence or title in a way that implies a relationship between them.

The digest as a whole should read as a coherent account of the week, not a list of unrelated summaries.
```
